title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about four people who talk about their sleep habits and the importance of rest. For questions 43–52, choose from the people (A–D). The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Rest and Repair: Four People on Sleep and the Importance of Rest

texts.A:
### Natasha, 37, Hospital Doctor
[43]As a doctor, I am acutely aware of the science behind sleep, yet for most of my twenties I treated my own sleep as something to be optimised out of existence.[/43] I was proud of getting by on five hours, which I now recognise was straightforwardly dangerous, both for me and for my patients. A health scare at thirty-two finally made me take rest seriously. [47]I now protect eight hours as non-negotiable, even when it means leaving tasks undone, and the difference in my decision-making and emotional regulation is stark.[/47] I speak to junior doctors about this whenever I can, because the culture of sleeplessness in medicine is deeply ingrained and genuinely harmful. [51]No degree of professional competence compensates for a brain that is running on insufficient sleep.[/51]
texts.B:
### Chris, 23, University Student
[44]I went through my first two years of university on almost no sleep and thought the constant low-grade exhaustion was just what student life felt like.[/44] It was only when I started tracking my sleep with an app that I realised how bad things actually were – I was averaging just over five hours a night. [48]Changing my sleep meant changing almost everything else: I stopped drinking alcohol midweek, cut out late-night gaming, and started going to bed at the same time every night, which my flatmates found hilarious.[/48] My grades improved significantly within one semester. [52]I'd never have believed beforehand that something as ordinary as a consistent bedtime could have such a measurable impact on my academic performance.[/52]
texts.C:
### Helen, 51, Retail Manager
I manage a team of forty people and for years I thought exhaustion was simply the price of responsibility. [45]My attitude to sleep changed completely when I noticed that my worst management decisions – the ones I later regretted – consistently happened when I was most sleep-deprived.[/45] [49]I began scheduling what I call a 'buffer hour' before bed – no work email, no news, just something genuinely restorative – and within weeks my mood at work was noticeably better.[/49] My team noticed the change before I did, which was both humbling and motivating. I now actively discourage a culture of late emails and long hours in my department, because I've seen what it costs people over time.
texts.D:
### Ahmed, 46, IT Consultant
[46]I went through a period of chronic insomnia during a particularly stressful project, and the experience left me with a lasting respect for how fragile the ability to sleep can be.[/46] I consulted a sleep specialist who helped me understand that most of what I'd been doing to try to sleep – lying in bed for hours, watching television to wind down – was actively making things worse. [50]Learning about sleep hygiene felt counterintuitive at first; some of the recommendations seemed so simple that I couldn't believe they would work.[/50] But they did. I now rarely struggle to fall asleep, and I notice immediately if I've started letting the healthy habits slip.

questions.question: admits to having ignored professional knowledge when it came to their own sleep habits?
questions.explanation: Natasha is a doctor who was aware of the science of sleep but treated her own sleep as something to be minimised throughout her twenties.

questions.question: did not realise how little sleep they were getting until they measured it?
questions.explanation: Chris thought constant exhaustion was normal student life until a sleep-tracking app showed he was averaging just over five hours a night.

questions.question: linked poor sleep directly to specific professional mistakes they later came to regret?
questions.explanation: Helen noticed that her worst management decisions consistently happened when she was most sleep-deprived.

questions.question: developed a lasting awareness of sleep's importance after a period of serious difficulty sleeping?
questions.explanation: Ahmed experienced chronic insomnia during a stressful project, leaving him with a lasting respect for how fragile sleep can be.

questions.question: now treats adequate sleep as an absolute priority even when work demands are high?
questions.explanation: Natasha protects eight hours as non-negotiable even when it means leaving tasks undone, and has noticed the difference in decision-making.

questions.question: describes making several lifestyle changes in order to improve their sleep?
questions.explanation: Chris stopped drinking midweek, cut out late-night gaming, and set a consistent bedtime in order to improve his sleep.

questions.question: introduced a regular pre-sleep activity that had a noticeable positive effect on their mood?
questions.explanation: Helen began a 'buffer hour' before bed with no work or news, and within weeks her mood at work improved noticeably.

questions.question: was initially sceptical that straightforward advice from a specialist could make a real difference?
questions.explanation: Ahmed found the sleep hygiene recommendations counterintuitive and couldn't believe such simple suggestions would work – but they did.

questions.question: makes a strong statement about the relationship between rest and professional ability?
questions.explanation: Natasha states that no degree of professional competence compensates for a brain running on insufficient sleep.

questions.question: expresses surprise at the impact a simple behavioural change had on their performance?
questions.explanation: Chris says he would never have believed that something as ordinary as a consistent bedtime could have such a measurable impact on his grades.
